raw_case:
Radiologic evaluation in the diagnosis of diplopia due to unilateral accessory extraocular muscle
This case report details a case of a 52-year-old female with a history of diplopia and an elevation deficit in her right eye. Initially misdiagnosed as an atavistic retractor bulbi muscle in 2005 via MRI, symptoms persisted despite surgical excision. Follow-up MRI in 2023 confirmed no changes, ruling out other causes like nerve palsy or schwannoma, and ultimately guiding diagnosis towards an accessory extraocular muscle. This case underscores the importance of imaging in diagnosing rare anatomical variations that may present with symptoms similar to more common conditions. Increased awareness of accessory extraocular muscles can enhance diagnostic accuracy and patient outcomes.

Introduction

The extraocular eye muscles (Fig. 1) are a mesenchymal-derived collection of 7 muscles that consist of the superior, inferior, medial, and lateral rectus muscles, the superior and inferior oblique muscles, and the levator palpebrae superioris [1]. With the exception of the levator palpebrae superioris, which elevates the eyelid, the extraocular eye muscles combine actions to allow for gaze in various directions [1]. The balance of tension produced by these muscles is essential in maintaining a normal primary gaze and for the development of normal binocular vision [1,2].In extremely rare cases, it is possible for there to be a supernumerary, accessory extraocular muscle present. In some cases, these accessory extraocular muscles may not manifest clinically; however, they may also present with significant symptoms including strabismus, diplopia, discomfort, gaze abnormalities, and reduced motility [3]. There are numerous, much more common conditions that may manifest similarly, emphasizing the importance of recognizing an accessory extraocular muscle as an etiology.Misalignment of the eyes is termed strabismus, and can be either comitant or incomitant [2,3]. Comitant strabismus typically affects infants and children and is defined by a constant angle of ocular misalignment in all fields of gaze [3]. On the other hand, incomitant strabismus is due to reduced ocular motility, which may be due to mechanical etiologies [3].Determination of the cause of strabismus is important, especially in infants and children, as this population is at risk for permanent deficits in binocular vision if alignment is not achieved within the critical period [4]. Etiologies for incomitant strabismus include cranial nerve palsies (III, IV, and VI), trauma, benign or malignant masses, Brown's syndrome, Duane's syndrome, and Graves’ disease [3,4]. The presence of an accessory extraocular muscle or fibrotic tissue may cause incomitant, restrictive strabismus [5]. Unfortunately, literature on accessory extraocular muscle as the cause of restrictive strabismus is rare, and furthermore, treatment for these patients is limited to surgery with varying degrees of corrective success [5].We present a case of a 52 year old female presenting with an elevation deficit in her right eye with orthotropic primary gaze. She has a past medical history of diplopia and was discovered to have an accessory extraocular right eye atavistic retractor bulbi muscle that was surgically excised in 2005. We describe the radiological characteristics of this orbital finding and discuss the importance of imaging studies in diagnosis of this rare anatomical variation.

Case presentation

A 52 year old female with a past medical history of double vision was found to have an atavistic retractor bulbi extraocular muscle on her right eye (Fig. 2). In 2005, the patient was evaluated for reports of diplopia. In order to investigate potential common causes such as cranial nerve palsy, an MRI was ordered. The MRI revealed the atavistic retractor bulbi extraocular muscle, which was determined to be the cause of the patient's symptoms.She then underwent surgery to excise the band. This did not improve motility but her double vision had been relatively stable in the primary position. At presentation in clinic in 2023 the patient was found to be orthotropic in the primary position with an elevation deficit in the right eye. At this point a follow up MRI of the orbits with gadolinium was performed to re-examine the patient condition, which showed no additional changes from the scan 18 years prior. This scan would also rule out other possible causes such as nerve palsy and schwannoma. Further surgery was not recommended.The exam performed was an MRI of the orbits without and with contrast on a G.E. Signa 1.5 Tesla MRI scanner. The globes were found to be unremarkable with no measured proptosis. Noted is a right orbital linear soft tissue lesion just medial to the medial rectus and parallel to it with enhancement pattern and signal intensity characteristics following extraocular muscle on all sequences (Fig. 2, Fig. 3). This was also seen on the previous MRI from 2005 and remains unchanged. The retroocular fat was clear. The optic nerve/sheath complexes were normal in configuration. The prechiasmatic optic nerves, optic chiasm, and optic tracts were unremarkable. There were no suprasellar or parasellar mass lesions. The cavernous sinuses showed normal configuration and enhancement. The superior ophthalmic veins showed normal caliber. The images of the whole brain showed no particular abnormality.

Discussion

This is a patient with a medical history of diplopia and a right accessory extraocular muscle, thought to be the atavistic retractor bulbi that was surgically excised in 2005, who presented with right ocular elevation deficit. Because the presence of this muscle is rare in humans, its function is not well understood. In other species, such as reptiles, this muscle retracts the globe, providing protection to the eye [5]. When present in humans, the composition of this remnant muscle is highly fibrotic, alluding to the idea that this muscle may exhibit minimal mechanical action, but due to its mass has the potential to disrupt movement and positioning of the eye within the orbit [5]. In addition to the mass of the muscle itself causing symptoms, the slight globe retraction this muscle provides can similarly result in vertical gaze restriction.It is well understood that a thorough history and physical exam are instrumental in determining the cause of vertical gaze diplopia. We demonstrate that in addition to careful history and physical exam, imaging studies are crucial in differentiating between causes of vertical strabismus, such as the rare accessory extraocular muscle, and other causes such as thyroid disease, cranial nerve IV palsy, and other orbital masses.Common causes of vertical diplopia include thyroid eye disease, myasthenia gravis, congenital cranial nerve IV palsy, trauma, and intracranial lesions causing cranial nerve palsy [3,4]. Less common causes include an orbital mass, with one of the most rare causes being an accessory extraocular muscle [4]. In creating a differential diagnosis for elevation deficit, it is important to consider an accessory extraocular muscle as the cause. This is especially important, because it has been demonstrated that these patients may be misdiagnosed as having Duane syndrome or congenital fibrosis of the extraocular muscle (CFEOM) [6]. This misdiagnosis prevents proper management and can result in persistence of elevation deficit, poor visual acuity, discomfort, and diplopia in these patients [6]. Clinical manifestation of an accessory extraocular muscle frequently occurs in childhood, along with numerous other congenital causes, such as Duane syndrome, Brown's syndrome, and CFEOM [3].The current literature emphasizes the importance of limiting unnecessary neurological imaging studies [5]. Still, when the cause of elevation deficit is less obvious, imaging, specifically MRI, should be performed to determine the presence of lesion, mass, or in rare cases, a fibrous or muscular accessory extraocular muscle. This will help differentiate between causes of elevation deficit that may require more immediate intervention. In most cases, however, urgent radiological studies are not necessary, unless trauma is the etiology [7]. Increased awareness of this condition may improve the radiologist's search pattern in evaluation of patients with a similar presentation [7].Most studies on the treatment of this condition shows that surgical excision for accessory extraocular muscles results in partial or no improvement of restrictive strabismus [6]. In the case of this patient, surgical excision was completed and was technically successful 18 years prior. Even so, the patient experiences similar restrictions in vertical duction of her right eye. Surgical approach typically involves identification and lysis of the accessory structure and possible recession of other rectus muscles [6]. In a case series of 7 patients with restrictive strabismus due to an accessory extraocular muscle, 5 of the patients underwent surgical correction, and partial improvement was achieved in only a single patient [6]. There are some single patient case reports suggesting an improvement in conditions following surgery in these patients [8]. While patients with an accessory extraocular muscle may be asymptomatic, it is important that for symptomatic patients, the benefits of surgery are not overstated.

Conclusion

This case highlights the role of radiologic evaluation in the diagnosis of a rare cause of vertical gaze deficit, which is typically monocular but can sometimes occur in both eyes. Inclusion of accessory extraocular muscle in a differential diagnosis for atypical strabismus is warranted, and can prevent misdiagnosing patients with other, more common conditions. Higher awareness of this anatomical variant will have 2 major impacts: improved search pattern of radiologists and better understanding of potential treatment options for these patients.

Patient consent

Consent was obtained from the patient for publication of this case report.

generate_case.case_summary:
- Patient Information: 52-year-old female 
- Chief Complaint: Diplopia and elevation deficit in the right eye.
- History of Present Illness: The patient has a persistent elevation deficit in the right eye. In 2005, she was diagnosed with an atavistic retractor bulbi accessory extraocular muscle via MRI, mistakenly considered the sole cause of her diplopia. She underwent surgical excision of the muscle, which did not improve motility, though her diplopia stabilized in the primary gaze.
- Past Medical History: History of diplopia since at least 2005.
- Personal History: None provided.
- Family History: None provided.
- Physical Examination: Orthotropic primary gaze with a persistent elevation deficit in the right eye upon follow-up in 2023.
- Ancillary Tests: MRI scans in 2005 and 2023 demonstrated the presence of a right orbital linear soft tissue lesion medial and parallel to the medial rectus, with signal intensity characteristics consistent with an accessory extraocular muscle. No changes were observed between the 2005 and 2023 imaging, and other conditions such as nerve palsy and orbital schwannoma were ruled out. The globes, optic nerves, cavernous sinuses, and other orbital structures were normal.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Differential Diagnoses:** Based on the patient’s chief complaints of diplopia and restricted vertical gaze, common and uncommon causes of incomitant strabismus were considered, including:
   - Thyroid eye disease
   - Myasthenia gravis
   - Congenital cranial nerve IV palsy
   - Trauma
   - Intracranial lesions causing cranial nerve palsy
   - Orbital masses or fibrotic lesions (e.g., schwannoma)
   - Rare anatomical variations such as an accessory extraocular muscle.

2. **Exclusion of Common Diagnoses:**
   - Thyroid eye disease: No clinical or radiological evidence supportive of this etiology.
   - Myasthenia gravis: No indication of fluctuating muscle weakness or other hallmark features.
   - Nerve palsy (III, IV, or VI): MRI findings showed no nerve compression or lesions.
   - Trauma: No history or imaging evidence.
   - Intracranial lesions: MRI of the orbits and brain was normal, excluding mass lesions or nerve involvement.
   - Schwannoma: MRI findings were inconsistent with this diagnosis.

3. **Diagnosis of Accessory Extraocular Muscle:**
   - MRI demonstrated a persistent, unchanged accessory extraocular muscle medial to the medial rectus, ruling it as the likely cause of restrictive strabismus and associated symptoms. 
   - Surgical excision in 2005 confirmed the presence of a fibrous mass compatible with an accessory muscle. Despite no improvement in motility post-surgery, the stability of her symptoms strongly supported this diagnosis over other potential causes.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Accessory extraocular muscle (atavistic retractor bulbi) causing restrictive strabismus with vertical gaze restriction and diplopia. This rare anatomical variant was confirmed by imaging and prior surgical evaluation.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Accessory extraocular muscle (atavistic retractor bulbi) causing restrictive strabismus and diplopia.

results.content:
### Reasoning:

Step 1: Review the patient's chief complaint and history of present illness.

The patient is a 52-year-old female who presents with diplopia and an elevation deficit in the right eye. This issue has been present since at least 2005 when she was diagnosed with an atavistic retractor bulbi accessory extraocular muscle via MRI. An atavistic retractor bulbi is a rare, vestigial muscle that can sometimes cause restrictions in eye movement if it attaches abnormally.

Step 2: Consider the treatment history and its outcomes.

In 2005, the patient underwent surgical excision of the accessory muscle, but this did not improve her motility issue, although her diplopia stabilized in the primary gaze. This suggests that the accessory muscle may not have been the sole cause of her symptoms or that its removal did not fully resolve the underlying problem.

Step 3: Evaluate the recent examination findings.

Upon follow-up in 2023, the patient still has a persistent elevation deficit in the right eye, with orthotropic primary gaze. This indicates that there is still a limitation in the upward movement of the right eye, which could be due to a weakness or restriction in the superior rectus muscle or other associated structures.

Step 4: Consider the ancillary test results.

MRI scans from both 2005 and 2023 show a right orbital linear soft tissue lesion medial and parallel to the medial rectus, consistent with an accessory extraocular muscle. There have been no changes in this lesion over the years, and other conditions like nerve palsy and orbital schwannoma have been ruled out. The normal appearance of the globes, optic nerves, cavernous sinuses, and other orbital structures suggests that the issue is specifically related to the extraocular muscles or their innervation.

Step 5: Rule out other possible causes.

Given that nerve palsy and orbital schwannoma have been ruled out, and the patient's history and imaging do not suggest inflammatory or vascular causes, the focus should remain on the extraocular muscles themselves. It's possible that there is a congenital anomaly or acquired condition affecting the superior rectus muscle in the right eye.

Step 6: Consider the impact of previous surgery.

The surgical excision of the accessory muscle in 2005 may have had unintended consequences, such as scarring or damage to surrounding tissues, which could contribute to the persistent elevation deficit. However, the MRI findings suggest that the accessory muscle remains present and unchanged since 2005.

Step 7: Synthesize the information to form a diagnosis.

Given the persistent elevation deficit in the right eye, the history of an accessory extraocular muscle, and the absence of other pathological findings, the most likely diagnosis is a restrictive strabismus due to an abnormal attachment or功能of the accessory muscle, possibly combined with scarring or fibrosis from previous surgery.

Alternative considerations could include superior rectus palsy, although nerve palsy has been ruled out. It's also possible that there is a mechanical restriction due to the accessory muscle's presence, even after surgical excision, perhaps due to residual tissue or fibrotic changes.

Step 8: Consider the need for further evaluation or intervention.

Given that the patient has had persistent symptoms despite previous surgery, and that the MRI shows no change in the accessory muscle, it may be necessary to consider further surgical intervention to address the elevation deficit. This could involve releasing any restrictive elements, including remnants of the accessory muscle or fibrotic tissue, and possibly performing strengthening procedures for the superior rectus muscle.

Additionally, a consultation with a strabismus specialist may be beneficial to assess the current eye alignment and motility, and to plan any potential surgical corrections.

### Answer:

The diagnosis is restrictive strabismus due to an abnormal accessory extraocular muscle and possible post-surgical fibrosis in the right eye.
